title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 5, with commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
author: attributed to Vālmīki (text); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta, with strong influence of idealist/Yogācāra-style monism)
genre: philosophical poem / didactic epic with commentary (bhāṣya/ṭīkā)
date_estimate: core text c. 9th-10th c. CE (some strata 6th-14th c.); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary much later (Ānandabodhendra, traditionally placed c. 16th-19th c.)
summary: The Yogavāsiṣṭha is a vast Advaitic verse dialogue (c. 23,000-32,000 verses) framed as the sage Vasiṣṭha instructing the young Rāma on the unreality of the phenomenal world, the nature of mind and consciousness, liberation while living (jīvanmukti), and the path of knowledge, illustrated by elaborate philosophical fables. Part 5 corresponds to a portion of the work (likely the Upaśama or Nirvāṇa prakaraṇa) treating the quieting of the mind and dissolution into the Absolute. This edition presents the Sanskrit verses together with the Tātparyaprakāśa, the standard learned gloss.
history: The work is pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki, author of the Rāmāyaṇa, but is a later composite Advaita text also called Mahārāmāyaṇa or Jñānavāsiṣṭha. The Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī became the most widely printed commentary, notably in the Nirṇaya-sāgara Press edition of Wāsudeva Laxman Śāstrī Paṇaśīkar.

title: Durgārcanasmṛti
author: Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Goswāmī
date_estimate: uncertain; likely late medieval / early modern (post-16th c. CE)
summary: A ritual-digest (smṛti) work setting out the procedures, rules, and liturgy for the worship (arcana) of the goddess Durgā, drawing on Purāṇic and Tantric-smārta sources. Such texts codify the sequence of pūjā, mantras, offerings, and observances for Durgā worship, especially around Navarātri/Durgā Pūjā.
history: An obscure ritual compendium attributed to one Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Goswāmī; little firm scholarly information about the author or precise date is available, and the work survives mainly in manuscript/printed Śākta ritual collections.

title: Mātṛkācakraviveka
author: Svatantrānandanātha (Svatantrānanda)
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism / Trika-Śākta; tantric phonematic mysticism)
date_estimate: medieval; uncertain, likely c. 13th-16th c. CE
summary: A treatise expounding the mātṛkācakra, the 'wheel of the mother-phonemes', i.e. the doctrine that the Sanskrit alphabet (the varṇamālā / mātṛkā) embodies the cosmic emanation of Śiva and Śakti, with vowels representing Śiva (prakāśa) and consonants representing Śakti (vimarśa). It analyzes how the phonemes, their groupings, and the kuṇḍalinī energy generate speech, mantra, and the manifested universe.
history: A specialized work in the non-dual Śaiva/Śākta phonematic-mysticism tradition associated with concepts elaborated by Abhinavagupta and later Krama-Trika authors; the author Svatantrānandanātha and the precise date are poorly attested in standard scholarship.

title: Sādhanamālā, Vol. 1
author: anonymous/traditional (compilation of many authors); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
date_estimate: individual sādhanas c. 7th-12th c. CE; this critical edition published 1925 (Gaekwad's Oriental Series No. 26)
summary: The Sādhanamālā ('garland of sādhanas') is a large anthology of several hundred Buddhist tantric sādhanas — visualization and ritual-worship texts for evoking Vajrayāna deities such as Avalokiteśvara, Tārā, Mañjuśrī, Hevajra, and many forms of Buddhas and Bodhisattvas. It is a primary source for Buddhist iconography and tantric liturgy. Volume 1 contains the first portion of the collected sādhanas.
history: Compiled from numerous authors over centuries, the anthology was critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya from eight manuscripts and published in the Gaekwad's Oriental Series (Vol. I, 1925). It is foundational to the modern study of Buddhist tantric art and ritual.

title: Sādhanamālā, Vol. 2
author: anonymous/traditional (compilation of many authors); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
date_estimate: individual sādhanas c. 7th-12th c. CE; this critical edition (Vol. II) published 1928 (Gaekwad's Oriental Series No. 41)
summary: The second volume of the Sādhanamālā, continuing the anthology of Buddhist tantric sādhanas for the visualization and worship of Vajrayāna deities, and including the editor's substantial introduction and apparatus. It remains a key reference for Buddhist tantric iconography and ritual practice.
history: Edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya and published as Gaekwad's Oriental Series Vol. 41 (1928); together with Vol. 1 it constitutes the standard edition of the collection.

title: Tripurārahasya, Jñānakhaṇḍa
author: attributed to the sage Hāritāyana (pseudepigraphic; 'Hāritāyana Saṃhitā')
tradition: Hindu (Śākta — Śrīvidyā / Tripurasundarī cult, with Advaita coloring)
date_estimate: medieval, c. 11th-17th c. CE (disputed)
summary: The Tripurārahasya ('secret of Tripurā/Tripurasundarī') is a Śākta work in three sections; this is the Jñānakhaṇḍa, the 'section on knowledge', comprising c. 2,000 verses on non-dual metaphysics, the nature of consciousness, and liberation, presented through the worship and philosophy of the goddess Tripurasundarī. It teaches an Advaitic-tantric path and is praised in the Ramana Maharshi tradition.
history: Ascribed to the legendary sage Hāritāyana (hence 'Hāritāyana Saṃhitā'); the standard learned commentary is the Tātparyadīpikā of Śrīnivāsa (1831 CE). The Jñānakhaṇḍa was edited and studied (English translation by A. U. Vasavada).

title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 6, with commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
author: attributed to Vālmīki (text); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
genre: philosophical poem / didactic epic with commentary
date_estimate: core text c. 9th-10th c. CE (composite); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary much later
summary: The sixth and final portion of the Yogavāsiṣṭha, corresponding to the Nirvāṇa-prakaraṇa, the longest section, which culminates the teaching with extended expositions of liberation, the dissolution of the mind into pure consciousness, and the state of the jīvanmukta, again richly illustrated with philosophical narratives. This volume gives the Sanskrit text with the Tātparyaprakāśa commentary.
history: Same as Part 5: the work is pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki, and the Tātparyaprakāśa of Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī is its principal commentary, preserved in the Nirṇaya-sāgara Press / Paṇaśīkar edition.

title: Śrīkarabhāṣya
author: Śrīpati Paṇḍitācārya (Śrīpati)
date_estimate: c. 14th-15th c. CE
summary: The Śrīkarabhāṣya is a commentary on the Brahmasūtras (Vedāntasūtras) of Bādarāyaṇa expounding the Vīraśaiva theological standpoint, often termed Śakti-Viśiṣṭādvaita or Vīraśaiva-Viśiṣṭādvaita. It argues a bhedābheda (difference-and-non-difference) relation between the soul/world and Śiva as supreme Brahman, and explicitly contests rival Vedānta readings, especially Rāmānuja's, while engaging Śaṅkara and Madhva.
history: Its author Śrīpati Paṇḍitācārya styled himself a Vīraśaiva ascetic; the work is the principal Vedāntic foundation for Vīraśaiva (Liṅgāyat) philosophy. It was discussed at length by historians of Indian philosophy such as S. N. Dasgupta.

title: Vijñānabhairava, with commentary Kaumadī (Kaumudī)
author: root text anonymous (revealed tantra); commentary Kaumudī by Bhaṭṭa Ānanda (Ānanda Bhaṭṭa)
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism — Trika; commentary by an Advaita-Vedāntin)
date_estimate: root text c. 7th-8th c. CE; Kaumudī commentary 17th-18th c. CE
summary: The Vijñānabhairava is a celebrated Trika tantra cast as a dialogue between Bhairava and the Goddess, presenting 112 dhāraṇās — concise contemplative/yogic techniques for realizing the non-dual consciousness (Bhairava) at the heart of all experience. This edition pairs the text with the Kaumudī commentary of Bhaṭṭa Ānanda, which reads it through a Śaṅkara-Advaita lens.
history: The Vijñānabhairava is a core scripture of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism, also commented on by Kṣemarāja and Śivopādhyāya. The Kaumudī of Bhaṭṭa Ānanda (whose colophon dates him to the 17th-18th c.) was published as Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies No. IX (1918).

title: Śivastotrāvalī
author: Utpaladeva (Utpalācārya)
date_estimate: c. early-to-mid 10th c. CE
summary: The Śivastotrāvalī ('series of hymns to Śiva') is a collection of devotional verses by Utpaladeva expressing intense bhakti toward Śiva while embodying the non-dual Pratyabhijñā philosophy of the recognition of one's own identity with Śiva. The hymns blend ecstatic devotion with the metaphysics of consciousness, and are valued both as poetry and as spiritual practice.
history: Utpaladeva, pupil of Somānanda and teacher in the lineage of Abhinavagupta, was the chief systematizer of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school. The Śivastotrāvalī was arranged and transmitted with a commentary by Kṣemarāja.

title: Śivasūtravimarśinī (text version) [commentary on the Śivasūtra revealed to Vasugupta]
author: Śivasūtra revealed to/by Vasugupta; Vimarśinī commentary by Kṣemarāja
date_estimate: Śivasūtra c. early 9th c. CE; Śivasūtravimarśinī c. early 11th c. CE
summary: The Śivasūtra is the foundational aphoristic scripture of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism, traditionally revealed to the sage Vasugupta, setting out the nature of universal Consciousness (Caitanya) as the Self, the means (upāyas) to liberation, and the powers of the realized yogin in three sections. The Śivasūtravimarśinī is Kṣemarāja's authoritative prose commentary; this is the text-only version.
history: Tradition holds that Vasugupta found the sūtras inscribed on a rock (Mahādeva mountain) by Śiva's grace. Kṣemarāja, a disciple of Abhinavagupta, produced the Vimarśinī, the standard commentary that frames the Śivasūtra within Trika-Pratyabhijñā doctrine.

title: Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya (text version)
author: Kṣemarāja
date_estimate: c. early 11th c. CE
summary: The Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya ('the heart of recognition') is Kṣemarāja's concise primer of the Pratyabhijñā philosophy, consisting of twenty short sūtras with his own auto-commentary. It distills the doctrine that the entire universe is the free self-manifestation of one absolute Consciousness (Citi/Śiva), and that liberation is the recognition (pratyabhijñā) of one's true nature as that Consciousness.
history: Written by Kṣemarāja as an accessible introduction to the more technical Pratyabhijñā works of Utpaladeva and Abhinavagupta; it remains the most popular entry-point text to non-dual Kashmir Śaivism and has been widely translated (e.g. by Jaideva Singh).

title: Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha, with commentary Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā
author: abridgement traditionally ascribed to Abhinanda (of Kashmir); commentary Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā by Ātmasukha (Mummaḍideva)
date_estimate: Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha c. 10th-11th c. CE; Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā commentary medieval
summary: The Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha ('shorter Yogavāsiṣṭha') is a condensed recension of the large Yogavāsiṣṭha, retaining its Advaitic teaching on the unreality of the world, the mind, and liberation-in-life through the Vasiṣṭha-Rāma dialogue, in a more manageable compass. This edition adds the Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā commentary.
history: The abridgement is traditionally credited to the Kashmiri Abhinanda; the Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā is the well-known commentary (by Ātmasukha / Mummaḍideva) printed with it. The Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha was historically more widely read and translated than the full text.

title: Spandakārikā, with Vivṛti by Rāmakaṇṭha
author: Spandakārikā by Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa (or Vasugupta); Vivṛti commentary by Rāmakaṇṭha
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism — Spanda school of non-dual Trika)
date_estimate: Spandakārikā c. 9th c. CE; Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti c. late 9th-10th c. CE
summary: The Spandakārikā ('verses on vibration') is the root text of the Spanda branch of Kashmir Śaivism, teaching that ultimate reality is a dynamic, throbbing Consciousness (spanda) — the creative pulsation of Śiva — and that recognizing this vibration in all states of experience is the means to liberation. This edition includes the Vivṛti, the commentary by Rāmakaṇṭha (Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha).
history: The kārikās are traditionally attributed either to Vasugupta or to his pupil Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa, and are closely linked to the Śivasūtra. Several commentaries exist (Kallaṭa's Vṛtti, Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti, and Kṣemarāja's Spandanirṇaya); Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti is one of the earliest.

title: Aniruddhasaṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional (revealed āgama)
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (c. 8th-13th c. CE range typical for Pāñcarātra saṃhitās)
summary: The Aniruddhasaṃhitā is a Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇava āgama of thirty-four chapters (c. 1800 granthas) prescribing temple ritual: modes of worship, initiation (dīkṣā), expiations (prāyaścitta), image-making and installation, and especially the annual temple festival cycle, to which it gives unusual attention, along with topics such as royal duty (rājadharma) and town planning.
history: One of the many (200+) Pāñcarātra saṃhitās, most preserved only in manuscript; named after Aniruddha, one of the vyūha (emanation) forms of Viṣṇu. It is a source for South Indian Vaiṣṇava temple liturgy.
related_works: Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā literature (e.g. Pādma, Pārameśvara, Sāttvata, Jayākhya saṃhitās)

title: Laghustuti, with commentary by Rāghavānanda
author: Laghubhaṭṭāraka (also a name used in the Krama/Śākta tradition); commentary by Rāghavānanda
date_estimate: medieval; stotra possibly c. 12th-15th c. CE, commentary later
summary: The Laghustuti ('short hymn') is a concise Śākta hymn praising the goddess (Tripurasundarī / Devī), dense with Śrīvidyā mantra-doctrine and the symbolism of the Śrīcakra, here accompanied by the explanatory commentary of Rāghavānanda. Such stutis function simultaneously as devotion and as encoded tantric teaching.
history: Attributed to Laghubhaṭṭāraka and published with Rāghavānanda's commentary in the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series; it belongs to the corpus of esoteric Śrīvidyā hymns whose precise authorship and dating are uncertain.

title: Pārānandasūtra
author: attributed to Pārānanda Muni (Parānanda)
date_estimate: early, c. 900 CE (one of the earlier surviving tantric sūtra works)
summary: The Pārānandasūtra is an aphoristic Tantric work expounding Kuladharma — described as the 'cream of the Vedas' — and teaching that the Supreme Being is one, while accommodating seven sectarian deities (Brahmā, Viṣṇu, Śiva, Gaṇeśa, Sūrya, Śakti, Bhairava). It treats the three paths of sādhana (Dakṣiṇa, Vāma, Uttara mārga) and stresses initiation (dīkṣā) from a qualified, ideally jīvanmukta, guru.
history: Regarded as one of the earlier extant Tantric sūtra texts (c. 900 CE), it is cited in surveys of Hindu Tantric/Śākta literature as an important witness to Kaula doctrine and its relation to Vedic authority.

title: Paraśambhumahimnastava (Paraśambhumahimnaḥstava)
author: attributed to one styled 'Krodhabhaṭṭāraka' (alias; the epithet is traditionally associated with the sage Durvāsas in the Krama tradition)
date_estimate: medieval; uncertain
summary: A hymn (mahimnastava) glorifying the 'greatness of the supreme Śambhu (Śiva)', composed in the non-dual Śaiva idiom in which praise of Śiva simultaneously articulates the recognition of one's own consciousness as Śiva. It belongs to the devotional-philosophical stotra literature of the Krama/Trika tradition.
history: Ascribed to an author bearing the epithet Krodhabhaṭṭāraka ('the wrathful lord'), a title traditionally linked to the sage Durvāsas, regarded in the Krama lineage as a transmitter of Bhairava Tantra. The work is obscure and not well documented in standard scholarship.

title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 1
author: attributed to Bharata Muni
tradition: Hindu (secular/scientific — śāstra on dramaturgy and the performing arts)
genre: śāstra (treatise on drama, dance, music, and aesthetics)
date_estimate: composite, c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (often c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE)
summary: The Nāṭyaśāstra is the foundational Sanskrit treatise on the performing arts, encyclopedically covering drama (nāṭya), dance, music, stagecraft, gesture, costume, the structure of plays, and above all the theory of aesthetic experience (rasa and bhāva). Volume 1 contains the opening chapters, including the mythic origin of theatre, the construction of the playhouse, and the foundational rasa/bhāva doctrine.
history: Ascribed to the legendary sage Bharata, it is in fact a composite work compiled over centuries. It is the single most influential text on Indian aesthetics, drama, and music, and generated a long commentarial tradition culminating in Abhinavagupta's Abhinavabhāratī.

title: Guhyasamājatantra
author: anonymous/traditional (revealed; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha/Vajradhara; sometimes associated with Asaṅga or with the mahāsiddha tradition)
date_estimate: c. 4th-8th c. CE; core often dated c. 4th-6th c. CE (disputed; commentarial Ārya school from c. 8th-9th c.)
summary: The Guhyasamājatantra ('tantra of the secret assembly/union') is one of the most important Buddhist Anuttarayoga (father) tantras, centered on the deity Akṣobhya/Guhyasamāja and the maṇḍala of the Five Buddhas. It systematizes the generation and completion stages of tantric yoga, deity visualization, mantra, and subtle-body practices, and is famous for its deliberately transgressive, antinomian symbolic language.
history: Considered the foundational text of its class, it is traditionally placed at the head of the Anuttarayoga corpus. It generated two major exegetical lineages — the Ārya (Nāgārjuna/Āryadeva attribution) and the Jñānapāda schools — and is central to later Indo-Tibetan Vajrayāna.
